proof_sketch: For the fixed input shape [128,4000] and the model parameter dim=1 (the likely dimension used, as inner_size must be 1 for the mutation to survive all tests), inner_size = product of sizes after dim = 1. The kernel launch is dim3 block(inner_size) = dim3(1), so there is exactly one thread per block. Both threadIdx.x and threadIdx.z are 0. Therefore, the original expression threadIdx.x and the mutated expression threadIdx.z always evaluate to the same constant 0. The mutation is semantically inert under the fixed-shape contract.

For the fixed input shape [128,4000], when cumulative product is performed along dimension 1, inner_size = 1, so block.x = 1. Consequently, there is only one thread per block and its threadIdx.z is 0, identical to threadIdx.x. The mutation cannot affect the computed values or memory accesses because the resulting inner_idx is always 0. All tested value distributions (including edge cases like near-overflow, denormals, alternating signs) produce identical bitwise output because the kernel behavior is unchanged. The only scenario that could expose the mutation would require inner_size > 1, but that would violate the fixed-shape contract.",

  "recommendations": "When writing mutation-robust kernels, include testing with at least two different dimensions or with inner_size > 1 to catch index-swap mutations. Additionally, consider using named constants or static assertions to verify that thread indices match the intended dimensionality."

Actually, all threads have threadIdx.z=0, so all threads will have inner_idx=0. But the block dimension is set as block.x = inner_size, so there are block.x * block.y * block.z = inner_size * 1 * 1 = inner_size threads total. All threads have threadIdx.z=0, so they all satisfy inner_idx < inner_size (since 0 < inner_size for inner_size>0). So all threads will execute the loop, but they all use the same inner_idx=0. So for a given outer_idx, all threads (which have same outer_idx because blockIdx.x is same) will calculate the same base_offset = outer_idx * dim_size * inner_size + 0. Since all threads compute the same product (because they all use inner_idx=0), the output for all inner indices (0..inner_size-1) will be the same?
